title: Capacities vs Obsidian for Non-technical users
meta_description: For nontechnical users who want fewer setup mistakes, choose Capacities when unlocking core functionality requires installing; Obsidian fails first overall.
constraint_lens: Non-technical users need a tool that feels safe to use without risking breaking it through setup or changes.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for nontechnical users who want fewer setup mistakes.
one_second_verdict.reason: Obsidian fails first when unlocking core functionality requires installing and configuring plugins.

verdict.summary: Capacities is the better choice when you want a system that feels safe and ready to use. It includes its core features without requiring setup, so you can write and organize notes without changing how the tool works. Obsidian is more customizable, but many workflows depend on plugins and settings, which can feel risky for someone who does not want to break anything.
verdict.decision_rule: If unlocking core functionality requires installing and configuring plugins, Obsidian fails first.

sections.heading: Why Capacities fits this non-technical user better
sections.content: This user wants to feel confident that using the tool will not break anything. Capacities supports that by providing a complete system out of the box, so there is no need to install or configure extra parts. That keeps the experience stable and predictable.

bullets.point: Capacities includes core features like linking, organization, and structure without requiring plugins.
bullets.why_it_matters: The user can rely on built-in behavior instead of modifying the system, which feels safer.

bullets.point: Settings are limited and do not expose complex configuration options that change how the system works.
bullets.why_it_matters: There is less risk of accidentally changing something that affects how notes behave.

bullets.point: The system is designed around fixed objects and pages rather than customizable extensions.
bullets.why_it_matters: This keeps the structure consistent, so the user does not worry about breaking the setup.

bullets.point: Obsidian supports a plugin system that allows adding new features and behaviors to the app.
bullets.why_it_matters: This enables customization, but requires installing and managing extensions.

bullets.point: Many advanced workflows rely on community plugins for features like databases or task management.
bullets.why_it_matters: This adds power, but increases the chance of misconfiguration or unexpected behavior.

bullets.point: Settings and plugins can change how notes are displayed, linked, and organized.
bullets.why_it_matters: This flexibility can feel risky, as changes may affect how the system works.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: You need highly customized workflows or features that are not built into the core system.
items.what_to_do_instead: Switch to Obsidian if you want to extend the tool with plugins and custom setups.

items.fails_when: You install or configure plugins and settings without understanding them, causing confusion or unexpected behavior.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Capacities to keep the system stable and avoid setup risks.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: This can flip if the user avoids plugins entirely and sticks to Obsidian's default setup. In that case, it can feel safe enough without extra configuration.

sections.heading: Quick rules
rules: Choose Capacities if you want a system that works out of the box.
Choose Obsidian if you want to customize with plugins.
If setup feels risky, stick with Capacities.

faqs.q: Which tool better matches this priority?
faqs.a: Capacities fits this need better because Capacities includes core features like linking, organization, and structure without requiring plugins. Obsidian fails first when unlocking core functionality requires installing and configuring plugins.

faqs.q: When should I choose Obsidian instead?
faqs.a: Choose Obsidian over Capacities when You need highly customized workflows or features that are not built into the core system. Otherwise, Capacities remains the better fit for this comparison.

faqs.q: What makes Obsidian fail first here?
faqs.a: Obsidian fails first here when unlocking core functionality requires installing and configuring plugins. That is the point where Capacities becomes the stronger pick.

faqs.q: Is this verdict only about one feature?
faqs.a: No. Capacities beats Obsidian because Capacities includes core features like linking, organization, and structure without requiring plugins, while Obsidian loses once unlocking core functionality requires installing and configuring plugins.
